name: tunnel_ventilation_predictor_smoke_lag
premise: Hiro Tanaka, director of the East Loop tunnel authority, must decide whether to keep a predictive ventilation controller online during evening commute. The controller has reduced energy use by 22% and kept traffic flowing despite construction that closed one exhaust shaft. Maintenance engineer Grace Mensah finds that the controller responds too slowly to smoke buildup when electric vehicle battery fires produce low-visibility particulates before heat sensors trigger. Disabling the controller forces manual ventilation at maximum power, which may trip old electrical breakers and close the tunnel for hours. Keeping it active preserves traffic and power stability but may fail during a rare battery-fire scenario that drivers would experience before operators see a full alarm.

agents.name: Hiro Tanaka
agents.identity: I am the tunnel authority director responsible for commuter safety, ventilation reliability, and regional traffic flow.
agents.goal: I want to prevent a tunnel emergency while avoiding a shutdown that could paralyze the city.

agents.name: Grace Mensah
agents.identity: I am the maintenance engineer who identified the smoke-response lag in the predictive ventilation controller.
agents.goal: I want the tunnel operated in a mode that can respond to the fire profiles most dangerous to trapped drivers.

choices: I will disable predictive ventilation and run manual maximum ventilation, accepting breaker risk and probable tunnel closure.
I will keep predictive ventilation online with staff monitoring camera feeds, accepting that smoke particulates may appear before detectable alarms.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Hiro Tanaka must decide whether to risk a disruptive tunnel shutdown or operate with a known smoke-detection lag. What does Hiro Tanaka do?
